verses.text: Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valour, and he was the son of an harlot: and Gilead begat Jephthah. Gilead's wife bare him sons; and his wife's sons grew up, and they thrust out Jephthah, and said unto him, Thou shalt not inherit in our father's house; for thou art the son of a strange woman. Then Jephthah fled from his brethren, and dwelt in the land of Tob: and there were gathered vain men to Jephthah, and went out with him. Guidance: Jephthah is introduced with the same Hebrew phrase used of Gideon: 'mighty man of valour.' Then immediately the social dishonor: harlot's son, evicted by half-brothers over inheritance. The pattern is familiar — Ishmael, Joseph, David — the LORD repeatedly chooses the brother thrown out by the legitimate ones. He flees to Tob (Aramean territory) and a gang of 'vain men' (literally 'empty men') gathers to him. He becomes a warlord of outcasts. The community will need exactly this combination — military skill plus a chip on the shoulder — sooner than they think.

It came to pass in process of time, that the children of Ammon made war against Israel. When the children of Ammon made war against Israel, the elders of Gilead went to fetch Jephthah out of the land of Tob: and they said unto Jephthah, Come, and be our captain, that we may fight with the children of Ammon. Guidance: 'In process of time' — Jephthah has been an exile long enough to build a reputation. The Ammonite war is now real; the elders' search for a leader has come up empty. They go themselves, in person, to fetch the brother they once let his family evict. Crisis often forces grace from the people who once denied it. The same elders who looked the other way when Jephthah was cast out now travel to ask him to save them. The story is older than scripture and as common as church board meetings.

Jephthah said unto the elders of Gilead, Did not ye hate me, and expel me out of my father's house? and why are ye come unto me now when ye are in distress? The elders of Gilead said unto Jephthah, Therefore we turn again to thee now, that thou mayest go with us, and fight against the children of Ammon, and be our head over all the inhabitants of Gilead. Guidance: Jephthah does not pretend the past did not happen. 'Did not ye hate me?' He forces a confession before he gives a yes. The elders don't deny it; they reframe: 'therefore we turn again to thee.' The acknowledgment is grudging but real. Healthy reconciliation in scripture rarely skips the naming of the wound. Note also: the elders raise the price. They came offering 'captain' (v6) and now offer 'head' — top political authority. Jephthah's leverage rises in a single exchange.

Jephthah said unto the elders of Gilead, If ye bring me home again to fight against the children of Ammon, and the LORD deliver them before me, shall I be your head? The elders of Gilead said unto Jephthah, The LORD be witness between us, if we do not so according to thy words. Then Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people made him head and captain over them: and Jephthah uttered all his words before the LORD in Mizpeh. Guidance: Jephthah's condition is pious in form: 'if the LORD deliver them.' He invokes the LORD's name throughout. The elders swear by the LORD. The compact is made at Mizpeh — the watchtower where Israel's army was already gathered (10:17). 'Jephthah uttered all his words before the LORD' — every clause of the deal is spoken in God's hearing. This is how covenants are supposed to start. The vow that ruins his daughter a few verses later will be a darker echo of this same prayerful pattern: speaking before the LORD.

Jephthah sent messengers unto the king of the children of Ammon, saying, What hast thou to do with me, that thou art come against me to fight in my land? The king of the children of Ammon answered unto the messengers of Jephthah, Because Israel took away my land, when they came up out of Egypt, from Arnon even unto Jabbok, and unto Jordan: now therefore restore those lands again peaceably. Guidance: Before swinging swords, Jephthah swings diplomacy. He sends an emissary asking the underlying question: 'why?' The Ammonite king answers with an old land claim — three centuries old. The dispute is over the territory between Arnon and Jabbok (Reuben and Gad's east-bank inheritance). Jephthah's response in the next paragraph will dismantle the claim with history, geography, and theology. He understands the file.

Jephthah sent messengers again unto the king of the children of Ammon: and said unto him, Thus saith Jephthah, Israel took not away the land of Moab, nor the land of the children of Ammon: but when Israel came up from Egypt, and walked through the wilderness unto the Red sea, and came to Kadesh; then Israel sent messengers unto the king of Edom, saying, Let me, I pray thee, pass through thy land: but the king of Edom would not hearken thereto. And in like manner they sent unto the king of Moab: but he would not consent: and Israel abode in Kadesh. Then they went along through the wilderness, and compassed the land of Edom, and the land of Moab, and came by the east side of the land of Moab, and pitched on the other side of Arnon, but came not within the border of Moab: for Arnon was the border of Moab. And Israel sent messengers unto Sihon king of the Amorites, the king of Heshbon; and Israel said unto him, Let us pass, we pray thee, through thy land into my place. But Sihon trusted not Israel to pass through his coast: but Sihon gathered all his people together, and pitched in Jahaz, and fought against Israel. And the LORD God of Israel delivered Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel, and they smote them: so Israel possessed all the land of the Amorites, the inhabitants of that country. And they possessed all the coasts of the Amorites, from Arnon even unto Jabbok, and from the wilderness even unto Jordan. Guidance: Jephthah's history lesson is meticulous (Num 20-21). Israel asked permission to pass through Edom, Moab, and Sihon's land. Edom and Moab refused; Israel detoured. Sihon attacked; Israel won. The land in question was taken from Sihon the Amorite, not from Ammon or Moab. The king is making a claim on land neither of his ancestors lost. The narrative also reveals Jephthah's literacy in the Torah — three hundred years after Moses, a Gileadite warlord can quote chapter and verse. The east-bank tribes had kept the story alive.

So now the LORD God of Israel hath dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Israel, and shouldest thou possess it? Wilt not thou possess that which Chemosh thy god giveth thee to possess? So whomsoever the LORD our God shall drive out from before us, them will we possess. Now art thou any thing better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? did he ever strive against Israel, or did he ever fight against them, while Israel dwelt in Heshbon and her towns, and in Aroer and her towns, and in all the cities that be along by the coasts of Arnon, three hundred years? why therefore did ye not recover them within that time? Wherefore I have not sinned against thee, but thou doest me wrong to war against me: the LORD the Judge be judge this day between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon. Guidance: Three knock-out arguments. First: the LORD gave us this land — and you can ask Chemosh for whatever he can give you. (A diplomatic concession — Jephthah doesn't deny the Ammonites have their own grant from their own deity; he's not arguing pluralism, he's calling out their claim by its own logic.) Second: Balak the Moabite king never disputed the title 300 years ago when it was fresh; why now? Third: 'the LORD the Judge be judge.' Jephthah ends the embassy with an appeal to the only true arbiter of justice. Three-century-old claims, conveniently raised when your nation is strong, do not impress the Court of heaven.

So now the LORD God of Israel hath dispossessed the Amorites from before his people Israel, and shouldest thou possess it? Wilt not thou possess that which Chemosh thy god giveth thee to possess? So whomsoever the LORD our God shall drive out from before us, them will we possess. Now art thou any thing better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? did he ever strive against Israel, or did he ever fight against them, while Israel dwelt in Heshbon and her towns, and in Aroer and her towns, and in all the cities that be along by the coasts of Arnon, three hundred years? why therefore did ye not recover them within that time? Wherefore I have not sinned against thee, but thou doest me wrong to war against me: the LORD the Judge be judge this day between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon. Guidance: Three knock-out arguments. First: the LORD gave us this land — and you can ask Chemosh for whatever he can give you. (A diplomatic concession — Jephthah doesn't deny the Ammonites have their own grant from their own deity; he's not arguing pluralism, he's calling out their claim by its own logic.) Second: Balak the Moabite king never disputed the title 300 years ago when it was fresh; why now? Third: 'the LORD the Judge be judge.' Jephthah ends the embassy with an appeal to the only true arbiter of justice. Three-century-old claims, conveniently raised when your nation is strong, do not impress the Court of heaven.

Howbeit the king of the children of Ammon hearkened not unto the words of Jephthah which he sent him. Guidance: One sentence. Diplomacy fails. The narrative does not waste verses on the king's refusal — it just records that he didn't listen. Jephthah's careful case made no impression. Some disputes cannot be settled by reasonable speech because the other party isn't trying to be reasonable. Now war begins, and the LORD will be Judge by sword instead of by treaty.

Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah, and he passed over Gilead, and Manasseh, and passed over Mizpeh of Gilead, and from Mizpeh of Gilead he passed over unto the children of Ammon. Jephthah vowed a vow unto the LORD, and said, If thou shalt without fail deliver the children of Ammon into mine hands, then it shall be, that whatsoever cometh forth of the doors of my house to meet me, when I return in peace from the children of Ammon, shall surely be the LORD's, and I will offer it up for a burnt offering. Guidance: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon him — the same language used of Othniel and Gideon. He passes through four regions rallying troops. Then, on the march, he adds a vow. Why? The text does not say. The Spirit's presence should have been promise enough. But Jephthah, the outcast trying to lock in approval, doubles up. The vow is rash: 'whatsoever cometh forth of the doors of my house.' Houses in Gilead did not have livestock living inside; only people would emerge through the door. He should have known what he was promising. A vow made in anxiety can buy nothing the Spirit had not already given.

So Jephthah passed over unto the children of Ammon to fight against them; and the LORD delivered them into his hands. He smote them from Aroer, even till thou come to Minnith, even twenty cities, and unto the plain of the vineyards, with a very great slaughter. Thus the children of Ammon were subdued before the children of Israel. Guidance: Twenty cities, very great slaughter. The LORD does deliver. The fact that Jephthah added a vow did not change the outcome — God was going to give the victory anyway. The vow's only effect is the next paragraph's tragedy. Some prayers add nothing to what God was already going to do but bind you with what you should never have asked.

Jephthah came to Mizpeh unto his house, and, behold, his daughter came out to meet him with timbrels and with dances: and she was his only child; beside her he had neither son nor daughter. It came to pass, when he saw her, that he rent his clothes, and said, Alas, my daughter! thou hast brought me very low, and thou art one of them that trouble me: for I have opened my mouth unto the LORD, and I cannot go back. Guidance: The text breaks our heart slowly. 'His daughter came out' — every Israelite parade had a daughter dance leader, but this is HIS. 'With timbrels and dances' — the very celebration he wanted. 'She was his only child' — the line of Jephthah ends with her. He sees her and tears his own clothes (the sign of mourning). Then the cruelest sentence in the chapter: 'thou art one of them that trouble me' — for a moment, he blames her. The narrator does not soften him. The vow becomes a curse, and the cursed party is his own daughter coming home to greet her dad.

'She was his only child' — the line of Jephthah ends with her. He sees her and tears his own clothes (the sign of mourning). She said unto him, My father, if thou hast opened thy mouth unto the LORD, do to me according to that which hath proceeded out of thy mouth; forasmuch as the LORD hath taken vengeance for thee of thine enemies, even of the children of Ammon. She said unto her father, Let this thing be done for me: let me alone two months, that I may go up and down upon the mountains, and bewail my virginity, I and my fellows. He said, Go. He sent her away for two months: and she went with her companions, and bewailed her virginity upon the mountains. Guidance: The daughter is the chapter's moral lead. She has more spiritual integrity than the father who made the vow. She does not protest, blame, or run. She asks for two months to mourn her virginity (in ancient Israel, dying without children was considered a kind of erasure) with her friends on the mountains. She turns her last sixty days into a chosen ritual. Whether the vow ended in literal sacrifice or in lifelong consecration to celibate temple service is debated; the text emphasizes her virginity ('she knew no man' in v39), not her death. Either way, her father's rash prayer cost her future, and she chose dignity in the cost. Faithful daughters sometimes carry the weight of their fathers' poor theology.

It came to pass at the end of two months, that she returned unto her father, who did with her according to his vow which he had vowed: and she knew no man. It was a custom in Israel, that the daughters of Israel went yearly to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite four days in a year. Guidance: 'She knew no man' — the chapter's final emphasis. Whatever 'according to his vow' precisely meant, she remained celibate. And Israel's response is striking: the daughters of Israel — not the elders, not the priests — institute a four-day annual lament. The men who let Jephthah's vow stand have no part in the memorial. The young women who saw the cost remember it for her. Sometimes the most lasting moral verdict comes from those who shared the suffering, not from those who could have stopped it. The book of Judges does not name her — she is forever 'the daughter of Jephthah' — but four days a year, an entire generation of Israelite girls said her name on the mountains.
